slides.mascot_explanation: First, we make our workspace ready! Put all the cotton balls in the left bowl, and place our tongs right in the middle so they're easy to reach.

slides.text: Demonstrate slowly how to squeeze the tongs to pick up one cotton ball and carefully transfer it from left bowl to right bowl.
slides.mascot_explanation: Watch carefully as we gently squeeze the tongs - not too hard or too soft - just right to pick up one cotton ball and move it to the other bowl. It's like giving the cotton ball a gentle hug with the tongs!

slides.prompt: Now it's your turn to use the tongs to transfer all the cotton balls from the left bowl to the right bowl, one at a time.
slides.mascot_explanation: Your turn to be the cotton ball mover! Take your time and move each fluffy ball one by one. If one falls down, that's okay - just pick it up and keep going!

items: Can you squeeze the tongs to pick up a cotton ball?

slides.text: Clean up materials together and return to shelf. Praise your child's concentration and careful movements.
slides.mastery_check: Child can independently use tongs to transfer cotton balls with developing pincer grip control
slides.mascot_explanation: What wonderful work you did today! Your fingers are getting so strong and steady. Let's put everything back where it belongs so it's ready for our next cotton ball adventure!

title: Sandpaper Letter 't': Tracing and Sound Practice with Touch Objects
description: Children explore the letter 't' through multisensory experience, tracing the sandpaper letter while learning its phonetic sound. They connect the letter to concrete objects that begin with the /t/ sound.
instructions: 1. Introduce the sandpaper letter 't' by showing the tactile letter card
2. Demonstrate proper finger tracing technique using index and middle fingers
3. Say the phonetic sound /t/ (not the letter name) while tracing
4. Guide your child to trace the letter three times, saying the sound each time
5. Present 3-4 objects that begin with /t/ sound (toy, top, tape, towel)
6. Have child trace the letter 't' then pick up each object, saying the sound
7. Practice the three-period lesson: 'This is /t/', 'Show me /t/', 'What is this?'
8. End with child tracing the letter independently while saying the sound
parent_notes: The sandpaper letters provide tactile reinforcement that helps children internalize letter formation through muscle memory. Always use the phonetic sound /t/ rather than the letter name 'tee' - this supports future phonetic reading. Allow your child to trace slowly and deliberately; the sensory experience is building neural pathways. If your child shows resistance, keep the session short and positive, focusing on the tactile exploration rather than perfection.

slides.heading: Sandpaper Letter 't'
slides.subheading: Tracing and Sound Practice with Touch Objects
slides.mascot_explanation: Hello! I'm Betsy Bear, and today we're going to meet a special letter that feels rough and bumpy under our fingers. The letter 't' makes the sound /t/ like 'top' and 'toy'!

slides.mascot_explanation: While we trace with our fingers, we make the sound /t/ - like the beginning of 'top' or 'toy'. Let's trace together and make that sound three times. /t/, /t/, /t/!

slides.prompt: Practice tracing the letter 't' and connecting it to objects that start with /t/. Use the three-period lesson format to reinforce learning.
slides.mascot_explanation: Now you get to be the letter detective! Trace the 't' with your fingers, then pick up each object that starts with /t/. Can you hear how they all start the same way as our letter?

items: Can you trace the letter 't' using two fingers?
Can you make the /t/ sound when you see the letter?

slides.mascot_explanation: Let me see your wonderful finger tracing! Can you show me how you make that /t/ sound? I love watching you discover which things start with our special letter sound!

slides.text: Have child trace the letter 't' independently while saying the sound. Store materials carefully and celebrate their learning.
slides.mastery_check: Child can trace letter 't' with proper finger technique and identify the /t/ sound
slides.mascot_explanation: You did such beautiful work learning about the letter 't' today! Your fingers traced so carefully, and you found all the /t/ treasures. Let's put our letter away safely for next time!

title: Number Rod 5 with Sandpaper Numeral: Counting Five Units Precisely
description: Children work specifically with the number rod representing 5, exploring its length through counting each unit segment while connecting it to the sandpaper numeral 5. This reinforces quantity recognition and numeral association.
instructions: 1. Present the red and blue striped number rod that represents 5
2. Place the rod horizontally and count each unit by pointing: 'one, two, three, four, five'
3. Introduce the sandpaper numeral 5, demonstrating proper tracing technique
4. Have your child trace the numeral 5 while saying 'five'
5. Return to the number rod 5 and count the units together again
6. Ask your child to match the sandpaper numeral 5 with the rod of 5 units
7. Practice counting the rod segments multiple times, emphasizing the quantity five
8. End by having child trace numeral 5 and count rod 5 independently
materials_needed: Number rod representing 5 (red and blue striped)
Small mat or tray for workspace
Pointer or small stick for counting
parent_notes: At this age, children are building concrete understanding of quantities before abstract number concepts. The combination of visual (seeing the rod), tactile (tracing the numeral), and kinesthetic (counting with movement) experiences helps embed the concept of 'five' through multiple pathways. Don't rush the counting process - allow your child to point to each segment deliberately. The sandpaper numeral provides muscle memory for eventual numeral writing while reinforcing the connection between quantity and symbol.

slides.heading: Number Rod 5 with Sandpaper Numeral
slides.subheading: Counting Five Units Precisely
slides.mascot_explanation: Hi friends! I'm Betsy Bear, and today we're going to explore the special rod that shows us exactly five units. We'll count each part and learn how to make the numeral 5 with our fingers!

items: Number rod representing 5 (red and blue striped)

slides.mascot_explanation: Now meet the numeral 5! It feels rough and bumpy under our fingers. Let's trace it carefully - down, around, and across - while we say 'five'. This is how we write the number that means five things!

slides.prompt: Count the units on the number rod 5 by pointing to each segment, then match it with the sandpaper numeral 5.
slides.mascot_explanation: Now you're the counter! Point to each stripe on the rod and count: one, two, three, four, five. Then put the numeral 5 right next to the rod to show they both mean the same amount!

slides.mastery_check: Child can accurately count five units on the rod and associate it with numeral 5

title: Finger Painting Winter Trees: Exploring Brown and White Color Blending
description: Children use their fingers to create winter tree scenes, exploring how brown and white paint blend together. This activity develops fine motor skills while encouraging creative expression through direct tactile paint experience.
instructions: 1. Cover work surface with newspaper or plastic tablecloth for protection
2. Set out large paper, brown and white finger paints, and damp cloth for cleanup
3. Show your child how to dip their finger in brown paint to make tree trunks and branches
4. Demonstrate making dots and strokes with fingertips for different textures
5. Introduce white paint for snow on branches and around trees
6. Encourage your child to experiment with mixing the colors on paper
7. Allow free exploration of finger painting techniques for winter scenes
8. Clean hands thoroughly and display artwork to dry
parent_notes: Finger painting provides crucial sensory experiences that support brain development and creativity. The direct tactile contact with paint helps children understand color mixing and texture in ways that brushes cannot provide. Focus on the process rather than the product - allow your child to explore freely without worrying about making recognizable objects. The goal is sensory exploration, fine motor development, and creative expression rather than artistic accuracy.

slides.subheading: Exploring Brown and White Color Blending

title: Brown Bear, Brown Bear Story Time: Color Recognition and Animal Sound Play
description: Children listen to the rhythmic, repetitive text of Brown Bear, Brown Bear, What Do You See? focusing on color words and animal names. This beloved book supports vocabulary development and pattern recognition through its predictable structure.
instructions: 1. Gather in a comfortable reading spot with good lighting
2. Show the book cover and read the title, pointing to each word
3. Ask your child to predict what animals they might see in the story
4. Read the book slowly, emphasizing the color words and animal names
5. Encourage your child to join in with the repetitive phrases they remember
6. After reading, discuss the different colors and animals from the story
7. Practice making some of the animal sounds from the book together
8. Ask your child which animal was their favorite and why
materials_needed: Brown Bear, Brown Bear, What Do You See? book
parent_notes: This predictable, repetitive text is perfect for building early literacy skills and confidence. The rhythm and pattern help children anticipate what comes next, supporting memory and language development. Don't worry if your child wants to 'read' along or recite parts from memory - this shows they're internalizing story structure and language patterns. The book's focus on colors and animals provides excellent vocabulary building opportunities while the repetitive structure makes children feel successful in their participation.

slides.heading: Brown Bear, Brown Bear Story Time
slides.subheading: Color Recognition and Animal Sound Play

items: Brown Bear, Brown Bear, What Do You See? book
